"Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen." The witnesses note the great description of faith. Gill notes the faith here is not a mere moral virtue, nor a bare assent to gospel truths, nor a faith of miracles, nor an implicit faith, nor a mere profession, but the true grace of faith. The witnesses note Paul describing faith as the substance of things hoped for and the evidence of things not seen; the twofold nature of faith — giving reality to hope and proof to the unseen — that Paul, opening his great chapter on faith, gives its working description: faith "is the substance of things hoped for" (it gives a present subsistence and reality to the future things we hope for, making them solid and present to the soul as if possessed), and "the evidence of things not seen" (it is the proof, the convincing demonstration, of unseen realities, so that the believer is as sure of them as if he saw them with his eyes); so that faith makes the future present and the invisible certain, laying hold of God's promises and the unseen world; faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen. The witnesses note the substance of things hoped for. The witnesses note the evidence of things not seen.

"For by it the elders obtained a good report." The witnesses note that the ancients were commended through faith. Gill notes by "the elders" are meant not merely old men, but those who lived in ancient times — some before the flood and to a great age, others in civil or ecclesiastical office, the ancestors of the Hebrews. The witnesses note Paul declaring that by faith the elders obtained a good report; the ancients commended by God through their faith — that Paul, before naming his examples, states the great point they will prove: "by it" — by this very faith just described — "the elders obtained a good report," that is, the men of old, the patriarchs and saints of former ages, were testified of and well reported, approved and commended by God himself, on account of their faith; so that it was their faith, and what it wrought, that won them God's testimony and an honoured name in the sacred record — the whole roll of witnesses that follows being proof that faith is the thing God commends; for by it the elders obtained a good report. "Through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear." The witnesses note that faith grasps the creation of the worlds by God's word. Chrysostom notes faith needs a generous and vigorous soul, rising above all things of sense and passing beyond the weakness of human reasonings, for one cannot become a believer otherwise than by raising oneself above common things. The witnesses note Enoch's translation by faith. "But without faith it is impossible to please him: for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him." The witnesses note that without faith none can please God. Gill notes without faith it is impossible to please God, or do anything well-pleasing in his sight, or any duty of religion acceptably — prayer, praise, attendance on the word — because such are without Christ and his Spirit. 33:
"Who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions," The witnesses note the great deeds wrought through faith. Gill notes "subdued kingdoms" as David did, who subdued Syria, Moab, Ammon, Amalek, Edom, and the Philistines; war in some cases is lawful, and faith makes use of means to subdue kingdoms. The witnesses note Paul declaring that through faith they subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, and stopped the mouths of lions; the mighty exploits achieved by the power of faith — that Paul, instead of telling each story, gathers the great achievements of faith into a series of swift, glorious strokes: "who through faith subdued kingdoms" (conquered hostile nations, as David), "wrought righteousness" (administered justice and lived godly lives, as the judges and kings), "obtained promises" (received the fulfilment of God's promised blessings), "stopped the mouths of lions" (as Daniel in the den, kept unharmed by faith); the witnesses note that every one of these triumphs is ascribed not to human strength but to faith — it was through faith that these men did valiantly and won what they won, faith being the secret of all their power; who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions. The witnesses note who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness. The witnesses note obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions.

The witnesses note the mighty exploits of faith — subduing kingdoms, working righteousness, obtaining promises, stopping the mouths of lions; every triumph ascribed not to strength but to faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the mighty exploits achieved by faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that the great triumphs of these men were won not by human strength but through faith. The witnesses note the subdued kingdoms: "who through faith subdued kingdoms" — Gill notes David subdued hostile nations; the subdued kingdoms; the David; the hostile nations; the conquered; the by faith. The expositors observe the wrought righteousness: "wrought righteousness" — administered justice, lived godly; the wrought righteousness; the justice; the godly; the the judges; the the kings. 34:
"Quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens." The witnesses note further triumphs of faith over fire, sword, and armies. Gill notes "quenched the violence of fire" — as when a believer is delivered out of it, as the three in the furnace, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, who were not hurt by it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that through faith they quenched fire, escaped the sword, out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant, and turned the enemy to flight; the deliverances and victories of faith over every danger — that Paul continues the catalogue of faith's triumphs: "quenched the violence of fire" (as the three Hebrews in the furnace, unharmed), "escaped the edge of the sword" (delivered from death by the sword), "out of weakness were made strong" (the feeble strengthened, as Samson, or Hezekiah raised from sickness), "waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens" (became mighty warriors and routed foreign armies); the witnesses note that these too were the fruit of faith — faith quenched flames, blunted swords, turned weakness into strength, and put whole armies to flight, so that there is no danger or enemy that faith, leaning on God, cannot overcome; quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, out of weakness were made strong. The witnesses note quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword. The witnesses note out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens.

The witnesses note faith's deliverances over every danger — quenching fire, escaping the sword, made strong out of weakness, routing armies; no danger that faith, leaning on God, cannot overcome.

So the witnesses make the verse the deliverances and victories of faith over every danger. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith quenched flames, escaped swords, turned weakness to strength, and routed armies. 35:
"Women received their dead raised to life again: and others were tortured, not accepting deliverance; that they might obtain a better resurrection:" The witnesses note both the deliverances and the sufferings of faith. Gill notes women received their dead raised to life again, as the widow of Zarephath and the Shunammite, whose sons were really dead and received alive again, by the faith of the prophets Elijah and Elisha. The witnesses note Paul declaring that women received their dead raised, and others were tortured, not accepting deliverance, that they might obtain a better resurrection; the turn from faith's triumphs to faith's sufferings, both crowned by resurrection hope — that Paul, having told faith's victories, now turns to faith's sufferings, marking the change with two contrasting cases: "women received their dead raised to life again" (the highest of deliverances — the dead given back, as by Elijah and Elisha), "and others were tortured, not accepting deliverance" (others, no less faithful, were broken on the rack and refused release at the price of denying God) — "that they might obtain a better resurrection"; the witnesses note the secret that nerved the martyrs — they scorned a deliverance that would cost their faith, choosing death because they looked for "a better resurrection," the glorious rising of the just, far better than any temporal rescue; that they might obtain a better resurrection. The witnesses note women received their dead raised to life again. The witnesses note others were tortured, not accepting deliverance; that they might obtain a better resurrection.

The witnesses note the turn from faith's triumphs to its sufferings — women receiving their dead raised, others tortured, refusing release, that they might obtain a better resurrection.

So the witnesses make the verse the turn to faith's sufferings crowned by resurrection hope. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith both raised the dead and nerved the martyrs to refuse deliverance for a better resurrection. 36:
"And others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment:" The witnesses note the cruel sufferings endured by the faithful. Gill notes others had trial of cruel mockings, as Samson by the Philistines, Elisha by the children, Jeremiah by Pashur, and the prophets whom God sent to the Jews as his messengers. The witnesses note Paul declaring that others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, of bonds and imprisonment; the shame and bonds borne by faith under persecution — that Paul continues the catalogue of faith's sufferings, descending to the harsher trials: "others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings" (were exposed to bitter mockery and the whip, as the prophets often were), "yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment"; the witnesses note that these faithful ones bore not only pain but shame — the cruel mocking that wounds the spirit, the scourging that tears the flesh, and the bonds and prison that rob of liberty — all endured by faith for God's sake; so that faith is proved not only in mighty deeds but in patient endurance of contempt, stripes, and chains; and others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment. The witnesses note others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings. The witnesses note yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment.

37:
"They were stoned, they were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword: they wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins; being destitute, afflicted, tormented;" The witnesses note the martyrdoms and destitution of the faithful. Chrysostom notes that, reflecting on the achievements of the saints, it comes over him to feel despondency concerning his own condition, since we have not even in dreams experienced the things among which those men spent their whole lives. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they were stoned, sawn asunder, tempted, slain with the sword, and wandered destitute, afflicted, and tormented; the extremity of suffering and death endured by faith — that Paul reaches the height of faith's afflictions, the very martyrdoms: "they were stoned" (as Zechariah and others), "they were sawn asunder" (as tradition says of Isaiah), "were tempted" (tried and solicited to forsake God), "were slain with the sword"; and beyond the killings, the lifelong destitution: "they wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins; being destitute, afflicted, tormented"; the witnesses note that these endured the worst the world could do — death in cruel forms, and a homeless, hunted, poverty-stricken life — yet held their faith through all; so that faith carries the believer through not only easy victories but the furnace of martyrdom and want; they were stoned, they were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword. The witnesses note they were stoned, they were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword. The witnesses note they wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins; being destitute, afflicted, tormented.

38:
"(Of whom the world was not worthy:) they wandered in deserts, and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth." The witnesses note that the world was not worthy of these afflicted saints. Gill notes these words are inserted in a parenthesis, to remove the objection that they were restless persons who made disturbance and were unfit to live in the world, or that they were justly punished; rather, the world was not worthy of them. The witnesses note Paul declaring, in a parenthesis, that the world was not worthy of these wandering saints who lived in deserts, mountains, dens, and caves; the surpassing worth of the persecuted faithful whom the world cast out — that Paul, in a striking aside, vindicates the honour of these despised sufferers: "(of whom the world was not worthy)" — far from being the worthless outcasts the world judged them, these hunted saints were too good for the world that drove them out; the world did not deserve to have such men in it — and Paul finishes the picture of their plight: "they wandered in deserts, and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth" (driven into the wilderness, sheltering in caves like hunted beasts); the witnesses note this reversal of the world's judgment — that those it counted refuse were its true treasure, the world being unworthy of them; of whom the world was not worthy. The witnesses note of whom the world was not worthy. The witnesses note they wandered in deserts, and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth.

39:
"And these all, having obtained a good report through faith, received not the promise:" The witnesses note that the faithful, though commended, did not receive the promise. Gill notes this may be limited to the sufferers in the preceding verses, who suffered martyrdom for the faith, and who are called martyrs or witnesses; or extended to all the elders mentioned in the chapter. The witnesses note Paul declaring that these all, having obtained a good report through faith, received not the promise; the faithful commended yet not yet given the full promise — that Paul gathers up the whole roll in one sweeping observation: "these all, having obtained a good report through faith" (every one of them, from Abel to the martyrs, won God's testimony and approval by their faith) "received not the promise"; the witnesses note the meaning — though they were all approved and commended, they did not in their lifetime receive the great promised thing (the coming of Christ and the full salvation he brings), the substance of all the promises, which was reserved for the gospel age; so that the Old Testament saints, for all their faith and their good report, looked and longed for what was not given until Christ came; and these all, having obtained a good report through faith, received not the promise. The witnesses note these all, having obtained a good report through faith. The witnesses note received not the promise.

The witnesses note the faithful commended yet not given the full promise — every one approved through faith, yet not receiving in life the great promised thing reserved for the gospel age.

So the witnesses make the verse the faithful commended yet not yet given the promise. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Old Testament saints, though approved through faith, did not receive in life the great promised thing. 40:
"God having provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect." The witnesses note that the saints of old and new are made perfect together in Christ. Gill notes this "better thing" is not a better state free from suffering, nor merely the felicity of the soul after death, but rather the better thing God provided in the coming of Christ and the gospel, in which the saints of all ages share together. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God provided some better thing for us, that they without us should not be made perfect; the one perfection shared by Old and New Testament saints together in Christ — that Paul closes the great chapter with a profound reason why the ancients received not the promise in their day: "God having provided some better thing for us" — God in his wisdom kept back the full blessing, the better thing of Christ's accomplished redemption and the gospel, for "us," the New Testament church — "that they without us should not be made perfect"; the witnesses note the glorious unity of this — the Old Testament saints were not to reach their final perfection apart from us, but all the people of God, of every age, are to be made perfect together, in one body, through the one Christ; so that the faithful of old waited for us, and all the redeemed share at last in one consummated salvation; that they without us should not be made perfect. The witnesses note God having provided some better thing for us. The witnesses note that they without us should not be made perfect.
